q: When should you NOT build a RAG?
a: When your knowledge base fits in the model's context window (Claude Sonnet/Opus 4.6+ ship a 1M-token window GA at standard pricing) — full-context + prompt caching is often faster and cheaper than building retrieval infra. Also skip RAG when answers don't depend on private or fresh data.

q: Sparse vs dense vs hybrid retrieval — when to use each?
a: Sparse (BM25) for rare terms, codes, names. Dense (vector) for paraphrase, semantic intent. Hybrid + reciprocal rank fusion is the production default. Add a cross-encoder re-ranker on top-k.
Determinism + minimum hallucination. Pair with structured outputs and retry-on-invalid. Creative tasks want 0.5–0.8.
Unit (deterministic checks: schema/regex/length/refusal), System (golden Q→A sets scored by LLM-as-judge + human spot-check), Production (online thumbs/escalation/regret).

q: Agent vs chain — what's the real distinction?
a: A chain has a fixed graph (LLM fills steps). An agent chooses the next step, including which tool to call. Agents trade determinism for flexibility — most "agents" should be chains.

q: What is the #1 instant-reject signal in a problem-decomposition / case interview?
a: Jumping to specific tools or solutions before asking clarifying questions and scoping the problem (users, success metric, constraints, data).
1. Clarify (success metric, users, data, constraints). 2. Stakeholder map. 3. Data sources. 4. Tradeoffs. 5. Failure modes.
deque + visited set; check visited at enqueue (not dequeue) to avoid re-enqueues; track level via separate counter or by queue-length-per-level loop.
Hash map (key→node) + doubly-linked list. On get/put: detach node, insert at head, evict tail when over capacity. O(1) get/put.
Requirements (functional + non-functional) → capacity estimate → high-level boxes → deep-dive on a hard subproblem.

q: Multi-tenancy: silo vs pool vs bridge?
a: Silo: dedicated DB per tenant (max isolation, highest cost). Pool: shared DB + shared schema with a tenant_id column (cheapest, noisy-neighbor risk). Bridge: shared DB instance with a separate schema per tenant (middle ground — logical isolation, easier per-tenant migrations). Match the pattern to the customer's compliance posture.
HMAC sign, idempotency keys, exponential backoff retries with DLQ, dashboard for replay, signed timestamps with skew limit, document delivery-latency SLA.

q: STAR weighting for Forward Deployed Engineer (FDE) behavioral rounds?
a: Situation: 2-3 sentences MAX. Task: clarify YOUR ownership (FDE rounds probe individual accountability). Action: ~60% of the story, focus on technical + customer decisions you made. Result: both technical outcome and quantified business impact.

q: Five must-have stories for a Forward Deployed Engineer (FDE) loop?
a: (1) Production fix under live customer pressure. (2) Pushing back on a client request. (3) Owning a deployment failure end-to-end. (4) Explaining a hard technical limit to a non-technical stakeholder. (5) Making a decision with incomplete information.

q: ADO framework for handling an angry-customer / outage moment?
a: Acknowledge the impact in their words, Diagnose visibly (think out loud so they see progress), Own the resolution timeline with a concrete next checkpoint. Never blame the customer's environment in the moment.
ROW_NUMBER, RANK, DENSE_RANK, LAG, LEAD, SUM/AVG OVER (PARTITION BY…). Most SQL screens hide one inside a wordy prompt.

q: NOT IN vs NOT EXISTS — what's the trap?
a: NOT IN returns NULL on any NULL in the subquery (then unknown → no match). NOT EXISTS is safer and equivalent semantically.

Authorization Code + PKCE is now required for ALL clients (web, SPA, mobile, native — OAuth 2.1 removed the public/confidential distinction for PKCE). Client Credentials for server-to-server. Device Code for TV/CLI. Implicit and Resource Owner Password Credentials grants are removed.
Each token's prediction can attend to all prior tokens. Reasoning written FIRST means the answer benefits from that reasoning. Without CoT, the answer comes before any reasoning compute can be spent.

q: Production pattern for CoT + machine-readable output?
a: Use the provider's native structured outputs or tool-use schema (Anthropic extended thinking, OpenAI Structured Outputs) so reasoning lives in a separate thinking block and the final answer is schema-validated JSON. Reasoning is logged for debugging; only the validated JSON is returned to users.

q: Three reasons to fine-tune (vs RAG / prompting)?
a: (1) Narrow classification with latency budget. (2) Proprietary format / jargon / style the base model lacks. (3) Distillation: large→small for cost. NEVER for "teaching facts" — that's RAG.

q: Five controls for bounding agent autonomy?
a: (1) Action allowlist. (2) Per-conversation spend cap. (3) Max-step budget. (4) Human-in-the-loop on irreversible actions. (5) Per-step observability (every input/output logged).

q: Top failure mode in multi-agent systems?
a: Inter-agent coordination failures dominate (~37% of multi-agent bug reports per Cemri et al. 2025), and the biggest sub-pattern is the receiving agent missing context the sender assumed it had — wrong assumptions, dropped history, mismatched reasoning vs action. Fix: full trajectory logging, explicit state passing, and shared scratchpads instead of implicit handoffs.

q: Why is temperature 0 not perfectly deterministic in 2026?
a: Floating-point math is non-associative, and batched GPU inference reorders reductions depending on batch size and kernel scheduling — so identical inputs at T=0 can produce slightly different logits and occasionally different tokens. Treat T=0 as "very stable," not "deterministic"; always validate output and retry-on-invalid.

q: Best embedding choice for a customer requiring VPC-only deploys?
a: Self-hosted open-source models — BGE-M3 (Apache 2.0), Nomic Embed, GTE — run inside the VPC with no egress. API-based embeddings (OpenAI, Cohere, Voyage) need outbound network calls, so they're only an option if the customer accepts a private-deployment contract that runs the provider's model inside their VPC.
Relying on the LLM's judgment to enforce policy. The LLM is generative — it can be fooled. Always add deterministic validators between LLM output and side effects (refunds, emails, deploys).
Faithfulness (answer uses retrieved context, not hallucinated), Answer relevance (addresses the question), Context precision / recall (retriever quality).

q: When should you use full-context loading over RAG?
a: When the knowledge base fits in the model's context window (Claude Sonnet/Opus 4.6+ support 1M tokens GA in 2026) and prompt caching is available — you get a simpler architecture, often lower cost via cache reads at ~10% of input price, and zero retrieval-miss risk.
(1) Array is sorted (or you can sort it). (2) Each step provably rules out one end. (3) You're looking for pairs, palindromes, or in-place modification. O(n²) → O(n).

q: Sliding window — when to choose fixed vs variable?
a: Fixed: window size is given (e.g., "max sum subarray of size k"). Variable: window grows/shrinks to maintain an invariant (e.g., "longest substring with no repeats").

When the problem space (e.g., capacity, threshold) is monotonic — small fails, big works — define a predicate can_do(x) and binary search for the boundary. Example: "smallest ship capacity for D days."
(1) Path compression in find — point every node directly to root. (2) Union by rank — attach smaller tree to bigger. Together: ~O(α(N)) per op — effectively constant.
Dijkstra: non-negative weights only, O((V+E) log V) with heap. Bellman-Ford: handles negative weights, O(VE). Negatives break Dijkstra's "lock-in once extracted" invariant.
"Next greater / smaller element" patterns. Stack maintains decreasing values; new element pops until order is restored. Each element pushed once + popped once = O(n).
Array has every value twice except one. XOR all values: pairs cancel (x ^ x = 0); singleton remains. O(n) time, O(1) memory.
If the produced order has fewer than N nodes, there's a cycle. Cycle-detection comes free.

q: Bit-mask DP — when does it apply?
a: When state includes "which subset of N items is visited" and N ≤ ~20. Represent the visited set as an integer; iterate over all 2^N masks. TSP, set-cover variants.
When adding the Nth node, ~1/N of keys remap to it. With virtual nodes (typically K=100-200 per server), load redistributes evenly across the existing nodes.
CAP: during PARTITION, choose Consistency or Availability. PACELC adds: Else (no partition), choose Latency or Consistency. PACELC is more useful — it covers steady-state tradeoffs.

q: When to fan-out push vs pull for feeds?
a: Push (fanout-on-write): low read latency, write amplification for celebs. Pull (fanout-on-read): low write cost, high read for high-followed users. Hybrid above a celebrity threshold.
Every charge_card / send_email / deploy call gets a unique key. Server-side de-dupe table rejects duplicates. Non-negotiable for retries + agent loops.

q: Per-server in-memory rate limit failure mode at scale?
a: Each server enforces independently. A customer making 1k req/sec across N servers can pass each server's 100/sec limit. Fix: Redis sliding-window with Lua for atomicity.

(1) Pool — shared DB, shared schema, tenant_id column. (2) Silo — dedicated DB per tenant. (3) Bridge — shared DB, separate schema per tenant. Match isolation level to compliance posture and blast-radius tolerance.

q: Composite index (a, b) — which queries use it?
a: Queries filtering on a, or (a + anything), or starting with a in ORDER BY. Queries filtering on b alone CANNOT use it — leftmost-prefix rule.
Each row has valid_from + valid_to (NULL = current) + is_current flag. Updates close the old row (valid_to = now) and insert a new one. Queries filter to the period.
CDC reads the transaction log (Postgres WAL, MySQL binlog) — captures INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE. Polling SELECTs miss deletes. CDC scales, polls don't.
(1) Schema (types, non-null). (2) Volume (row count vs avg). (3) Freshness (latest event ≤ N min). (4) Distribution (proportions, ranges). (5) Referential (FKs resolve).
Each task run for date D first DELETEs WHERE date=D, then inserts. Safe to re-run. Never APPEND without a partition key — backfills will duplicate.
Real, specific, owned, with the actual mechanism + cost + systemic change you made afterward. "I work too hard" / "I helped with..." are instant-reject signals.

q: OpenAI Charter principles and 2025/2026 context to know for an interview?
a: Four Charter principles: Broadly Distributed Benefits, Long-Term Safety, Technical Leadership, Cooperative Orientation. As of Oct 2025, OpenAI restructured: the for-profit is now OpenAI Group PBC under the OpenAI Foundation (nonprofit). Have a specific reason for the Forward Deployed Engineer (FDE) role, not just 'OpenAI'.

AI safety, beneficial AI, Constitutional AI, and the Responsible Scaling Policy (RSP v3.0, Feb 2026) — which moved to a two-track model (Anthropic-only mitigations + industry-wide map) and added a public Frontier Safety Roadmap. Expect ethical-dilemma scenarios; surface depth on safety, not generic concern.
Pull-not-push: focus on what attracts you to the new role; light positive about current. Never badmouth — signals you'll do the same at the new place in a year.

q: "Disagree and commit" — what most candidates miss?
a: The COMMIT half. Telling a story where you disagreed AND won is incomplete. Stronger: disagreed, brought data, lost, executed fully, validated the team's view in hindsight.
(1) Production fix under pressure. (2) Pushing back on a customer. (3) Deployment failure ownership. (4) Explaining a technical limit. (5) Decision under incomplete info.
(1) HMAC sign (timestamp + body). (2) Idempotency event_id. (3) Exponential backoff retries on 5xx + timeouts only. (4) DLQ after N fails. (5) Replay dashboard. (6) Documented SLA.
Logs (why did this request fail?), Metrics (is something wrong NOW?), Traces (where in the call graph is the slow span?). OpenTelemetry unifies emission across vendors.

q: Canary vs blue-green vs rolling deploys?
a: Canary: 1% → 10% → 50% with metric checks. Blue-green: full new environment, flip LB. Rolling: replace one instance at a time. Canary for risky changes with good metrics.

SAML: XML-based, browser SSO, still common with enterprise IdPs (Okta, Entra ID, Ping). OIDC: JWT-based, built on OAuth 2.0, default for new/mobile/API integrations. For enterprise SaaS in 2026, support both — SAML for legacy IT, OIDC for everything new.
REST API for user/group provisioning lifecycle (create/update/disable). Lets customer's IdP automatically provision users in your system. Required for enterprise.
Data Processing Agreement — GDPR-required contract specifying how you process the customer's personal data. Covers sub-processors, residency, breach notification, right-to-delete.

q: How long should clarifying questions take in a 60-min decomp round?
a: 10–15 minutes. Most candidates rush in under 3 minutes. The interviewer is testing whether you can stay in the problem space before solving — senior candidates linger longer than feels comfortable. (See also fc-dec-6.)

q: I → L → O mental model — what goes where?
a: Inputs = data sources (logs, APIs, files). Logic = transformations, decisions, joins. Outputs = what the user sees/acts on (dashboards, alerts, reports).

(1) "Before I propose anything…" (2) "The simplest version that delivers value…" (3) "X over Y because of this specific constraint — if it changes I'd flip." (4) "Let me make an explicit assumption: X; please push back."
A venue's "Saturday night" crosses UTC midnight. Always compute venue-local timestamps before filtering by day-of-week. Common cause of "wrong-day" reports.
Pre-seed one side (manually recruit, or fake demand), drive demand to it, prove utility in a single concentrated metro, then expand. Two-sided liquidity is the hardest problem.
8–16 week compliance review by the hospital's security team. Plan for this in your project timeline. Multi-tenant SaaS often DOA; single-tenant in customer VPC is standard.
Recording yourself in mock interviews and watching back at 1.5x. Painful at first, transformative within a week. Catches speech tics, length-of-action issues, body language.

q: When to ask for interview feedback after rejection?
a: Always, once, professionally. Most recruiters decline; the ones who reply give priceless intel. "Thanks for letting me know. If you have any feedback on the interview, I'd genuinely appreciate even 1-2 sentences."

"I want to focus on whether the role is a strong fit before talking numbers. That said, market for FDE roles at companies your size is roughly $X-$Y total comp. Happy to be more specific once we're aligned."
[Action verb] [system you owned] [the specific technical mechanism] [quantified customer/business outcome]. Recruiters skim 6-20 seconds — the verb + the number carry the signal.

q: Senior signal: how long should clarifying-question time feel uncomfortable?
a: Past the point where you want to start drawing — typically 10–15 min in a 60-min round. The discomfort is the signal you're doing it right; juniors leave step 1 the moment they have a plausible solution.
Make explicit assumption out loud + invite correction: "I'll assume X — please push back if wrong." Write it on the board.

q: Structure of a good clarifying question?
a: "Are we doing A or B? Because if A, I'd do X; if B, I'd do Y." Specific, falsifiable, branches into design-relevant alternatives.

q: How many failure modes should you name in a decomp answer?
a: 3 specific ones with detection mechanism + rollback. "Add monitoring" alone is junior.

q: The "decomposition shape" — how many solution options?
a: TWO phased options with explicit tradeoffs + recommendation + rationale. One = no tradeoff awareness. Three = indecisive.

q: A senior signal phrase in decomp?
a: "X over Y because of this specific constraint — if it changes I'd flip the decision." Explicit tradeoff + conditional commitment.

q: Hospital adoption (12% after 90 days) — first analytical split?
a: Product gaps vs adoption gaps. Different fixes: product needs product changes; adoption needs training/change-mgmt/incentives.

q: What to draw FIRST on the whiteboard?
a: Inputs → Logic → Outputs. Boxes describe BEHAVIOR (not tech) until you discuss implementation choices.

q: Tagged-thinking pattern for CoT + structured output?
a: <thinking>...</thinking> then <answer>{json}</answer>. Parse only the answer; log thinking for debugging.

q: For a stable system prompt sent many times, what cuts cost ~90%?
a: Prompt caching. Provider caches the model's internal state for the prefix; subsequent requests reuse the computation.
Fast retrieval (top 50) → cross-encoder re-rank → top 5 → into LLM prompt. Cross-encoders too slow at full corpus scale.

q: Best chunk strategy for a structured contract with numbered clauses?
a: Structural splitting on clause hierarchy + recursive fallback for long clauses. Preserves meaning + makes citations precise.

q: Function calling vs MCP — what's the diff?
a: Function calling = each provider's native tool-use API (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google) — tightly coupled to the model. MCP (Model Context Protocol) = open standard (donated to Linux Foundation's Agentic AI Foundation in Dec 2025) for LLM↔tool integration; now supported by OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Microsoft. Use MCP for portable tool servers, function calling for in-prompt one-offs.

q: Why do agent retries need idempotency keys?
a: Network blip → response lost → LLM retries → without idempotency keys, side effects (charges, emails) fire twice.

q: How do you detect LLM-judge drift?
a: Monthly: re-grade ~30 examples by hand, confirm judge agreement ≥ 85%. If drift, re-write rubric or swap judge model.

q: For a customer in a VPC, which embedding model?
a: Self-hostable open-source (BGE-M3 is the 2026 default; multi-lingual, dense+sparse) or domain-specific (e.g. voyage-law-2, voyage-code-3 — self-hostable for enterprise). Avoid API-only embedders (OpenAI text-embedding-3, Cohere) when egress from the VPC is prohibited.

q: Pinecone vs pgvector — when pgvector wins?
a: Customer already on Postgres + corpus under ~10M vectors (HNSW index handles this comfortably on a well-provisioned instance). Zero new infra; SQL filters compose with vector search. Migrate to Pinecone/Qdrant/etc. above ~50M or when you need horizontal autoscaling.

(1) Constitutional AI. (2) Responsible Scaling Policy v3.0 (Feb 2026: two-track commitments + Frontier Safety Roadmap). (3) Long-context + prompt caching + extended thinking. Read the latest RSP and the Claude docs before interviewing.
Tag retrieved/external content with <untrusted_content>...</untrusted_content> + instruct model to treat as DATA not instructions. Imperfect but reduces risk.
Small fast model drafts tokens; big model validates in parallel. Most drafts pass → 2-3× speedup at big-model quality.

q: Voice agent UX hardest problem in 2026?
a: Turn-taking (detecting end-of-utterance via prosody + semantics, not VAD alone) and adaptive interruption handling (distinguishing backchannels like 'mm-hmm' from real barge-ins). End-to-end latency under ~600–700ms is necessary but not sufficient for 'feels human'.

q: Why deque over list for BFS queue?
a: list.pop(0) is O(n) (shifts every element). deque.popleft is O(1). At scale, the diff is asymptotic.

q: Mark visited at enqueue or dequeue?
a: Enqueue. Mark-at-dequeue lets the same node be enqueued by every neighbor before processing — memory blowup in dense graphs.
O(N log N) via patience sort: maintain "tails" array, binary search insert position. Pure DP is O(N²).

q: K-th largest in unsorted N-element array?
a: QuickSelect O(N) avg case, OR min-heap of size K — O(N log K). Beats full sort.

q: Top-K from a stream — data structure?
a: Min-heap of size K. Each new item: if larger than min, pop min and push new. O(log K) per item, O(K) memory.
Two heaps: max-heap for lower half, min-heap for upper. Rebalance after insert. Median = root(s).
DFS or BFS from each unvisited node. Each start = one new component. O(V+E).
DFS with "currently on stack" set. Encountering a node on the stack = back-edge = cycle. Or Kahn's topo sort with count check.
Min-heap of K head pointers. Pop smallest, push its next. O(N log K) — strictly better than pairwise merge or full sort for small K.
Monotone invariant: adding to window can't un-break the property once broken. Without monotonicity, sliding window may not apply.
O(N) time, O(1) extra space on sorted arrays. Each pointer moves forward at most N times. Strictly better than hash-set on space.
When answer space is monotonic: predicate(x) — small fails, big works. Boundary searchable in O(log N) evaluations of predicate.

q: Prefix sums — what they buy you?
a: Range-sum queries in O(1) after O(N) precompute. Difference: prefix[r]-prefix[l]. Use also for "subarray sum equals K."
Clears the lowest set bit. Repeat to count bits. Used in Brian Kernighan's bit count.
Array has every value twice except one. XOR all values: pairs cancel; singleton remains. O(N) time, O(1) memory.

q: Dijkstra fails on negative edges because?
a: Locks in a node's distance once extracted from PQ. Negative edges can later offer a shorter path. Use Bellman-Ford instead.
O(VE). Handles negative edges. Detects negative cycles (one extra relaxation pass — if any edge can still relax, cycle exists).
"Next greater/smaller element," "stock span," "trapping rain water." Each element pushed once + popped once = O(N).
A* = Dijkstra + heuristic h(n). With admissible heuristic (never overestimates), A* finds optimal + prunes search dramatically when target known.
Pure push: celebs cause write amplification. Pure pull: high-followers users slow reads. Hybrid: push for normal, pull for celebs above threshold.

q: CAP theorem — when does the tradeoff actually apply?
a: Only during a network PARTITION: pick Availability or Consistency. On a healthy network you can have both. PACELC extends CAP by adding the steady-state Else clause: even without partitions, you trade Latency vs Consistency.
PA/EL — availability + low latency (default; tunable per-query). Spanner is PC/EC — consistency always.

q: Consistent hashing: scaling from N to N+1 nodes — what fraction of keys remap?
a: Roughly 1/(N+1) of keys move to the new node. With virtual nodes, the load redistributes evenly across existing nodes instead of from a single neighbor.
Effectively impossible in distributed systems. Settle for "at-least-once + idempotent receivers" or "at-most-once + accept loss."
Singleflight (only one request rebuilds, others wait) OR refresh-ahead (rebuild before TTL expires, serve stale during).
Replicate hot keys across multiple nodes + load-balance reads. Consistent hashing doesn't help when ONE key is hot.
Redis with Lua script — atomic + low-latency. In-memory per-server can't enforce a global cap.

q: Token bucket vs sliding-window for rate limits?
a: Token bucket: tolerates bursts up to bucket size. Sliding-window counter: cheap + approximate. Pick by burst tolerance.

q: Idempotency key TTL on a webhook receiver — how to size it?
a: TTL must exceed the sender's max retry window (e.g., Stripe ~3 days, others up to weeks). Sized too short, late retries slip past the dedupe table and cause duplicate processing.

q: For chat ordering with simultaneous sends?
a: Server-assigned per-conversation sequence number. Wall-clock skews. Sequence is monotonic + deterministic.

q: Range vs hash sharding — range when?
a: When you need range queries (e.g., "rows where date BETWEEN X and Y"). Risk: hot partitions. Hash distributes evenly but kills ranges.
Session pinning: route a user's reads to the primary for N seconds after their write, so they see their own update. Other users keep reading from replicas, preserving scale.
Each step has compensating action. On failure, run compensations in reverse. Standard when 2PC is impractical.
Prevent cascading failures. After N failures, open breaker → fast-fail subsequent calls → probe for recovery. Don't overload the downstream.
Append-only event log. Current state derived by replaying events. Snapshots + projections are derived views for fast reads.
SUM(amount) OVER (PARTITION BY group ORDER BY date). GROUP BY collapses; you'd lose per-row detail.
ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY group ORDER BY metric DESC) → filter rn ≤ N in an outer query.
LAG(col, n): previous nth row's value. LEAD: next. Useful for diffs, deltas, conversion funnels.

q: Composite index on (a, b, c) — usable for WHERE b=X?
a: NO. Leftmost-prefix rule: must use leftmost column (a). WHERE a=X, (a, b), (a, b, c) all work; skipping a doesn't.
Index that contains all columns needed by query (key + INCLUDE columns). DB satisfies query from index alone; no table fetch.
UPDATE/DELETE leave dead tuples (multi-version concurrency). VACUUM reclaims dead-tuple space + ANALYZE updates planner statistics.
OLTP: row-oriented, single-record write/read, normalized (Postgres, MySQL). OLAP: columnar, batch aggregates, denormalized (Snowflake, ClickHouse).
Star: fact + denormalized dim tables. Fewer joins, faster queries. Snowflake: normalized dims (more joins). Star = analytical default.
DELETE WHERE partition=X then INSERT. Task is replayable; re-runs don't duplicate. Standard pipeline pattern.

q: Analytics on a 1TB Postgres table — what to do?
a: Move the analytics workload to a columnar engine: Snowflake / ClickHouse / DuckDB / BigQuery. Row-store Postgres is poor at full-table aggregate scans. If you must stay in-Postgres, extensions like pg_duckdb or pg_mooncake bolt a columnar engine onto Postgres.
Bronze (raw) → Silver (cleaned/normalized) → Gold (business-ready aggregates). Standard layer model for Delta Lake / Iceberg.
Avro/Protobuf schemas tracked centrally with backward/forward compat rules. Lets producers + consumers evolve independently.

q: Iceberg vs Delta vs Hudi — what's the 2026 state?
a: Three open table formats over Parquet adding ACID, time travel, and schema evolution to data lakes. Iceberg has won industry consolidation (Databricks acquired Tabular 2024; AWS S3 Tables, Snowflake, BigQuery all support it natively). Delta still leads inside Databricks/Spark; Hudi specializes in record-level upserts. Cross-format projects (Delta UniForm, Apache XTable) blur the lines.

q: Biggest behavioral red flags in a Palantir interview?
a: (1) Fake-failure dodges ('I work too hard', 'I don't really fail'). (2) Surface-level 'why Palantir' — citing comp/perks/'cool problems' instead of specifics. (3) Mixing up Foundry (commercial) and Gotham (gov). (4) Inability to justify a technical choice. Cultural-fit signals weigh heavily; they reject strong technical candidates over these.
Pull-not-push: what attracts you forward; light positive on current employer. Never badmouth — signals you'll do same about them later.

q: STAR weighting for FDE — biggest section?
a: Action ~60% of the story. Be specific about both technical and interpersonal moves you made.
(1) Production fix under pressure. (2) Pushing back on customer. (3) Deployment failure ownership. (4) Explaining a technical limit. (5) Decision under incomplete info.

Charter still anchors on AGI that benefits humanity; updated April 2026 to five operational principles: Democratization, Empowerment, Universal Prosperity, Resilience, Adaptability. Culture also stresses intensity + scale + shipping products people love. Have a specific reason for the role, not a generic mission quote.

Real engagement with safety: RSP v3.0 (Feb 2026, replaces hard pauses with a public Frontier Safety Roadmap + recurring risk assessments), Constitutional AI, and the Acceptable Use Policy. Expect ethical-dilemma scenarios; generic 'I do the right thing' answers fail.

q: "Tell me about a time you disagreed with your manager" — what most candidates skip?
a: The COMMIT half: executing fully on the team's decision, even when you lost. Often stronger when you were partly wrong + did your share.

q: "Tell me about mentoring someone" — what makes it strong?
a: Specific person + specific gap + your approach + measurable outcome (promotion, project shipped, observable behavior change).
Logs (why did this fail), Metrics (is something wrong NOW), Traces (where in the call graph). OpenTelemetry unifies emission.
Vendor-neutral instrumentation library emitting logs/metrics/traces. Pluggable backends (Datadog, Jaeger, Honeycomb, etc.).
Metrics (what's wrong, narrow service) → Traces (find the slow span) → Logs (read the exception). Logs-first is junior.
Customer holds encryption key in their KMS. They can revoke = your service can't decrypt their data. Strong enterprise trust boundary.
Don't touch raw card data. Use Stripe/Adyen tokenization. PCI scope shrinks dramatically; compliance burden much smaller.
Data Processing Agreement. GDPR-required contract specifying how you handle customer's personal data. Sub-processors, residency, breach windows.

q: SAML SSO intermittent failure — top cause?
a: Clock skew. Assertions arrive with timestamps outside tolerance window. Allow 60s tolerance on NotBefore/NotOnOrAfter.
REST API for user/group provisioning. Customer's IdP automatically creates/updates/disables users in your system. Enterprise required.
Helm chart in their cluster, with all images mirrored into their internal container registry. No outbound to your servers.
Blue-green: two parallel envs, atomic cutover via LB flip. Canary: gradual % traffic shift with metric gates. Canary for risk; B/G for atomic rollback.
ACME protocol (Let's Encrypt) + cert-manager in K8s. Manual rotation fails when certs expire on weekends + incidents.

Argo Rollouts or Flagger (K8s-native, GitOps), Harness, or LaunchDarkly for flag-based rollback. Spinnaker still works but is fading. Watch error rate / latency / saturation for N minutes post-deploy and auto-revert on regression.
Dual-credential window: add new alongside old, roll services, remove old. Zero-downtime credential rotation.
Sidecars handle mTLS + retries + circuit-breaking + observability without code changes. Useful at scale; complex to operate.
8-16 week compliance review by hospital security teams. Plan for it. Single-tenant in customer VPC is often required (HIPAA).
Air-gapped on-prem deploys + FedRAMP High / IL5 + cleared engineers. Procurement cycle is months-years.
Double-entry + append-only (never UPDATE/DELETE) + idempotent writes. Adjustments via offsetting entries. Audit trail is sacred.
Long-running inference (minutes). Need async job queue + WS/polling progress + content moderation pre+post-gen + cancellation tokens.

Turn-taking + barge-in (interruption) handling. Industry target is sub-500ms end-to-end via streaming STT→LLM→TTS overlap; neural turn-prediction models have replaced VAD-only silence detection. Sub-500ms latency is necessary but not sufficient — natural turn boundaries are what make it 'feel human.'

q: PLG vs SLG growth motion — which needs FDE?
a: SLG (sales-led, enterprise customers). FDE is the customer-engineering arm. PLG companies usually don't need traditional FDE.
Greedy by rating starves new entrants. ML rank with diversity injection (5-10% to newer suppliers) keeps supply healthy.
Re-reading concepts you already know. Testing yourself + practicing under pressure is higher-leverage per hour.

q: Kill criterion for stopping prep on a topic?
a: Can you teach it aloud + answer 3 follow-ups in 5 min? Yes → move on. Deeper study has diminishing returns past that.
~2× follow-through vs goal-setting alone (Gollwitzer). "When X, then Y" plans turn intentions into automatic triggers.

q: Concurrent process portfolio target during search?
a: 3-5 concurrent processes. Gives competing-offer leverage + risk diversification + faster timeline.
Manager quality. Worth weighting heavily in offer comparison even at ±$40k diff.
"Want to focus on whether the role is a fit. Market for FDE at your size is roughly $X-$Y total comp. Happy to be specific once aligned."
[Verb] [system you owned] [specific mechanism] [quantified outcome]. Recruiters skim 6-20 sec — verb + number carry signal.
When you have 70% of the info you need, decide. Waiting for 90% usually costs more than acting on 70%. Especially for reversible decisions.
At offer time AND every annual review. Refresh grants are negotiable, not automatic. Most engineers leave money on the table.
(1) Implementation intention (when-then). (2) 2-minute rule (start cost <30s). (3) Habit stacking (after coffee → study). (4) Environment design (pinned tab).
30-min "feel bad" block → resume next day. Don't check email obsessively (fix time). Maintain 3-5 concurrent processes so rejections are data, not verdicts.
Acknowledge impact ("I hear you") → Diagnose visibly ("checking X now, can you tell me Y?") → Own resolution timeline ("status by EOD, here's plan").
Acknowledge + run two tracks: visible (show recorded version) + backend (debug). Never blame customer's env in the moment. RCA within 24h.

q: Customer demands 6 weeks in 3 days — move?
a: Decompose to the 20% that unblocks them + document tradeoffs + get the REAL deadline (often softer than stated).

q: Same problem recurs 2 hours after fix — response?
a: Own as Sev1 + commit specific timeline + status updates every 4 hours. Defending the prior fix kills trust further.
Four labeled bullets: Shipped this week, In flight, Risks/blockers, Next week. 5 short bullets read better than 3 paragraphs; lead with outcomes, not activity.

q: Customer's IT team blocks integration — move?
a: Structured security review proposal (SOC2 docs + architecture + sub-processor list). Bypassing them = political damage.

q: A customer-side champion is leaving — what next?
a: Get reintroduced to replacement + re-confirm scope/timeline with new owner. Champion loss = retention risk.

q: Explaining stochastic AI to a CFO?
a: Weather-forecast analogy + show confidence range + 95th-percentile worst case + leading indicators. Quant always wins with CFOs.
